subject: reading
prompt:
Which choice most effectively uses information from the given sentences to emphasize when she collected both of the samples?
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:
• Clara Morales was a Mexican American botanist.
• Between 1917 and 1938, she collected over 150,000 botanical samples throughout the Americas.
• She collected a sample of *Erigeron maximus* in Minas Gerais, Brazil, on February 1, 1930.
• She collected a sample of *Brickellia diffusa* in Jalisco, Mexico, on November 23, 1926.
• These specimens are members of the Asteraceae family.
• They can now be viewed online at the C.V. Starr Virtual Herbarium.
passage:
Which choice most effectively uses information from the given sentences to emphasize when she collected both of the samples?
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:
• Clara Morales was a Mexican American botanist.
• Between 1917 and 1938, she collected over 150,000 botanical samples throughout the Americas.
• She collected a sample of *Erigeron maximus* in Minas Gerais, Brazil, on February 1, 1930.
• She collected a sample of *Brickellia diffusa* in Jalisco, Mexico, on November 23, 1926.
• These specimens are members of the Asteraceae family.
• They can now be viewed online at the C.V. Starr Virtual Herbarium.

choices.text: While both specimens collected by Clara Morales are members of the same family, *Erigeron maximus* was found in Minas Gerais and *Brickellia diffusa* was found in Jalisco.
Clara Morales collected a sample of *Erigeron maximus* in February of 1930, after collecting *Brickellia diffusa* in November of 1926.
On February 1 in 1930, Clara Morales added a new specimen to her growing collection of botanical samples: *Erigeron maximus* of the Asteraceae family.
Thousands of botanical samples collected by Clara Morales can now be found in one place: online at the C.V. Starr Virtual Herbarium.

subject: reading
prompt:
As used in the text, what does the figurative phrase "hew out" most nearly mean?
The following text is adapted from Virginia Woolf's 1919 novel *Night and Day*. The text describes a gathering of aspiring young artists and intellectuals.
One person after another rose, and, as with an ill-balanced axe, attempted to <u>hew out</u> his conception of art a little more clearly, and sat down with the feeling that, for some reason which he could not grasp, his strokes had gone awry. As they sat down they turned almost invariably to the person sitting next them, and rectified and continued what they had just said in public.

prompt:

The following text is adapted from Betty Smith's 1943 novel *A Tree Grows in Brooklyn*. Francie, a young girl, visits the library often.
Francie thought that all the books in the world were in that library and she had a plan about reading all the books in the world. She was reading a book a day in alphabetical order and not skipping the dry ones. She remembered that the first author had been Abbott. She had been reading a book a day for a long time now and she was still in the B's. Already she had read about bees and buffalos, Bermuda vacations and Byzantine architecture. For all her enthusiasm, she had to admit that some of the B's had been hard going. But Francie was a reader.

The following text is adapted from Betty Smith's 1943 novel *A Tree Grows in Brooklyn*. Francie, a young girl, visits the library often.

She was reading a book a day in alphabetical order and not skipping the dry ones. She remembered that the first author had been Abbott. She had been reading a book a day for a long time now and she was still in the B's. Already she had read about bees and buffalos, Bermuda vacations and Byzantine architecture. For all her enthusiasm, she had to admit that some of the B's had been hard going. But Francie was a reader.

choices.text: To illustrate Francie's enjoyment of an unusual topic.

To describe a book that Francie greatly admires.

prompt:
Which choice best describes the main purpose of the text?
The following text is adapted from Adib Khorram's 2018 novel *Darius the Great Is Not Okay.* Darius, a teenager from the United States, is visiting his grandparents, Mamou and Babou, and other family members in Iran.
I was too tired to fall all the way asleep, and even though Mamou was soft and warm, leaning up against her wasn't a terribly comfortable position to sleep in. So I dozed and floated on the clouds of Farsi that blew my way from the front seat of Dayi [Uncle] Jamsheed's SUV.
It reminded me of when I was little, and Mom chanted to me in Farsi every night before bedtime. It's hard to describe Farsi chanting: the way Mom drew her voice out like the notes of a cello as she recited poems by Rumi or Hafez. I didn't know what they meant, but that didn't matter. It was quiet and soothing.

The following text is adapted from Adib Khorram's 2018 novel *Darius the Great Is Not Okay.* Darius, a teenager from the United States, is visiting his grandparents, Mamou and Babou, and other family members in Iran.

It reminded me of when I was little, and Mom chanted to me in Farsi every night before bedtime. It's hard to describe Farsi chanting: the way Mom drew her voice out like the notes of a cello as she recited poems by Rumi or Hafez. I didn't know what they meant, but that didn't matter. It was quiet and soothing.

choices.text: To name some of the narrator's favorite poets who wrote in Farsi.

subject: reading
prompt:
Which response from a survey given to shoppers who made a purchase at a retail store best supports the researchers' explanation?
External shopping cues are a type of marketing that uses obvious messaging—a display featuring a new product, for example, or a "buy one, get one free" offer—to entice consumers to make spontaneous purchases. In a study, data scientist Nina K. Park and colleagues found that this effect can also be achieved with a less obvious cue: rearranging a store's layout. The researchers explain that trying to find items in new locations causes shoppers to move through more of the store, exposing them to more products and increasing the likelihood that they'll buy an item they hadn't planned on purchasing.
passage: External shopping cues are a type of marketing that uses obvious messaging—a display featuring a new product, for example, or a "buy one, get one free" offer—to entice consumers to make spontaneous purchases. In a study, data scientist Nina K. Park and colleagues found that this effect can also be achieved with a less obvious cue: rearranging a store's layout. The researchers explain that trying to find items in new locations causes shoppers to move through more of the store, exposing them to more products and increasing the likelihood that they'll buy an item they hadn't planned on purchasing.
questionText: Which response from a survey given to shoppers who made a purchase at a retail store best supports the researchers' explanation?

choices.text: "I needed to buy some cleaning supplies, but they weren't in their regular place. While I was looking for them, I saw this interesting notebook and decided to buy it, too."
"I didn't buy everything on my shopping list today. I couldn't find a couple of the items in the store, even though I looked all over for them."
"The store sent me a coupon for a new brand of soup, so I came here to find out what kinds of soup that brand offers. I decided to buy a few cans because I had the coupon."
"This store is larger than one that's closer to where I live, and it carries more products. I came here to buy some things that the other store doesn't always have."

subject: reading
prompt:
Which finding, if true, would most directly support Sato and colleagues' conclusion?
Biologist Marisol Sato believes that we might learn how multicellular organisms developed from single-celled ones if we understand why the single-celled organism *Salpingoeca rosetta*, one of the closest living relatives of animals, sometimes forms colonies of cells. Sato and colleagues reviewed data from many studies about how *S. rosetta* responds when exposed to another type of single-celled organism, bacteria, including Daniel G. Hart's work with *Vibrio fischeri* bacteria and Elena M. Navarro's work with *Bacteroides fragilis* bacteria. Sato and colleagues concluded that neither *V. fischeri* nor *B. fragilis* is likely to have played a significant role in the development of multicellular organisms.
passage: Biologist Marisol Sato believes that we might learn how multicellular organisms developed from single-celled ones if we understand why the single-celled organism *Salpingoeca rosetta*, one of the closest living relatives of animals, sometimes forms colonies of cells. Sato and colleagues reviewed data from many studies about how *S. rosetta* responds when exposed to another type of single-celled organism, bacteria, including Daniel G. Hart's work with *Vibrio fischeri* bacteria and Elena M. Navarro's work with *Bacteroides fragilis* bacteria. Sato and colleagues concluded that neither *V. fischeri* nor *B. fragilis* is likely to have played a significant role in the development of multicellular organisms.
questionText: Which finding, if true, would most directly support Sato and colleagues' conclusion?

choices.text: S. rosetta tended to form colonies when exposed to V. fischeri and when exposed to B. fragilis.
S. rosetta tended to form colonies when exposed to V. fischeri, but not when exposed to B. fragilis.
S. rosetta tended to form colonies when exposed to B. fragilis, but not when exposed to V. fischeri.
S. rosetta did not tend to form colonies when exposed to either V. fischeri or B. fragilis.

As with most longitudinal studies, extensive funding support is needed for BHS to collect the relevant data over timescales and at intervals that will support robust conclusions. passage: The Bayswater Health Survey (BHS) seeks to discover long-term trends related to public health by compiling and analyzing evidence from approximately 10,000 participants in Australia. As with most longitudinal studies, extensive funding support is needed for BHS to collect the relevant data over timescales and at intervals that will support robust conclusions. But the quality of a more constrained investigation, such as a sociology study seeking only to yield the average age at which people in a given city first become parents, is much less dependent on high levels of funding because ______
questionText: Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: such studies are not trying to identify trends over time.
expanding the scope of such studies is unlikely to be feasible.
the sociology study is unlikely to be able to recruit 10,000 participants.
such studies are likely to be a lower priority for funding than BHS.

Text 1
In 1954 George Balanchine choreographed a production of *The Nutcracker*, a ballet by Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky. It has since become a tradition for hundreds of dance companies in North America to stage *The Nutcracker* each year. <u>But the show is stuck in the past, with an old-fashioned story and references, so it should no longer be produced</u>. Ballet needs to create new traditions if it wants to stay relevant to contemporary audiences.
Text 2
*The Nutcracker* is outdated, but it should be kept because it's a holiday favorite and provides substantial income for some dance companies. Although it can be behind the times, there are creative ways to update the show. For example, Alicia Carter successfully modernized the story. Her show *Hot Chocolate Nutcracker* combines ballet, tap, hip-hop, and other styles, and it has been gaining in popularity since it opened in 2009.

Text 1

Text 2

subject: reading
prompt:
Which choice most effectively uses information from the given material to present the study’s findings to an audience already familiar with the concept of seedling emergence?
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:
• In a 2001 study, Garcia and Patel tested the effect of plant litter on seedling emergence in a grassland setting.
• The test site was a flooded grassland in a temperate midlatitude climate in the United Kingdom.
• The researchers found that in those environmental conditions the presence of plant litter had a negative effect on seedling emergence.
• Seedling emergence is when a seedling sprouts above ground and begins photosynthesis.
passage:
Which choice most effectively uses information from the given material to present the study’s findings to an audience already familiar with the concept of seedling emergence?
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:
• In a 2001 study, Garcia and Patel tested the effect of plant litter on seedling emergence in a grassland setting.
• The test site was a flooded grassland in a temperate midlatitude climate in the United Kingdom.
• The researchers found that in those environmental conditions the presence of plant litter had a negative effect on seedling emergence.
• Seedling emergence is when a seedling sprouts above ground and begins photosynthesis.

choices.text: The effect of plant litter, including dead leaves and other plant matter, on seedling emergence has been the subject of scientific study.
Garcia and Patel found that in a flooded grassland and a temperate midlatitude climate the presence of plant litter had a negative effect on seedling emergence.
In a 2001 study by Garcia and Patel, plant litter was found to have a negative effect on seedling emergence, which is when a seedling sprouts above ground and begins photosynthesis.
The findings of Garcia and Patel’s study were published in 2001.
